What This Album Is About

The System
Is Failing.

The Controlled Menu

If you have ever stood in a voting booth and felt like you were choosing between two options someone else picked — you were right. Wake Up opens with that feeling and refuses to let it go. Illusion of Choice and Divide and Rule name what most people sense but have never heard said plainly: the fight between left and right is largely managed. When the cameras go off, the same donors write the checks. The same island hosts the same guests. The anger you feel is real — but who taught you who to aim it at?

The Money That Decides Before You Vote

In 2010, the Citizens United ruling eliminated century-old limits on corporate political spending. Since then, outside groups have poured over $9 billion into federal elections — with roughly $3 of every $10 coming from donors whose identities are never disclosed. In the 2024 election alone, super PACs spent a record $2.7 billion. Money doesn't just amplify voices — it decides which voices exist at all. Candidates without that backing don't lose. They disappear before you ever hear their name.

The Playbook They Already Published

JP 3-13.2 is not a metaphor — it is a real U.S. Department of Defense joint publication. The official doctrine for Military Information Support Operations: what the Pentagon rebranded from PSYOP in 2010. It describes in precise detail how to micro-target audiences, tailor different messages to different groups, use trusted local messengers to spread narratives, manufacture the appearance of consensus, and produce "observable, measurable behavioral responses." The song names these tactics verbatim — not as conspiracy, but as documented government methodology — and asks one question: what happens when those tools point inward?

The Children No One Is Talking About

The FBI has designated the 764 network a Tier 1 terrorism investigation — the same category as ISIS. It is an international predator network targeting children between the ages of 8 and 17 on platforms like Roblox, Discord, and Snapchat. Members groom children into producing abuse material, then blackmail them into self-harm, violence, and worse — streamed live for an audience. A 13-year-old named Jay died by suicide after a 764 member pushed him to take his own life. As of 2025 the FBI had over 350 active investigations. The DOJ has charged at least 30 people. The song 764 does not look away. It gives voice to the survivors' right to be seen and names the perpetrators for exactly what they are.

The Island Everyone Knows About and No One Names

Epstein Island — Let Them Rot is the album's most direct confrontation. Jeffrey Epstein's operation on Little Saint James was not a secret — it was a protected system. Powerful men from finance, politics, science, and entertainment visited. Evidence was gathered. Silence was purchased. When prosecution finally came, it came decades late. Many of those who participated have faced no consequence. This track names what that silence is: complicity — not just by the participants, but by every institution that knew and chose quiet. It makes a vow to the survivors and a promise to the guilty.

The Religion That Became a Lock and Key

Real Talk with Jordan and State Power, War Propaganda, and Religious Control address something that crosses every political line: the use of faith as a tool of control. When devotion is weaponized into obedience, when questions are punished, when God is made to look like a distant judge counting your failures — that is not theology. That is management. Yahweh Surround Me holds the counter: faith stripped of performance, rebuilt on presence. The album does not reject God. It refuses to let institutions own Him.

The Attention You Never Agreed to Sell

Cambridge Analytica harvested the data of 87 million Facebook users without consent — quizzes, personality tests, music preference surveys — and used the resulting psychological profiles to micro-target voters. Algorithmic newsfeeds are tuned not for information but for engagement, and engagement means rage. The Federal Reserve adjusts rates that determine whether millions can afford their rent. These are not abstract forces. They are daily. The album names them not to send you into despair but to hand you the map. Power is not mystical. It is paperwork, payroll, access, and complicit quiet. You can learn to read it.

What Comes After Waking Up

Rebuild closes the record without a fist in the air. It is a prayer and a working plan. Plant questions in the young. Feed the hungry places. Build kitchens and schools that don't sell the soul. Show up for the neighbor you've been taught to call the enemy. The most dangerous thing to those who hoard power is not a protest — it is a people who remember how to talk to one another. This is a July 4th record. Not because America is exceptional. Because the promise of what it could be is still worth fighting for — with witnesses, with lists, with light.

The Mission

Not Fury.
Focus.

Lazarus Grimm lives where nu-metal's raw, uncompromising power meets conscious hip-hop's unflinching truth-telling. Think For Today meets Rage Against The Machine — but with a flashlight instead of a torch, and a list instead of a grievance.

The same forces that teach you to hate your neighbor, that rent your anger back to you with ads on top, that carve your shared home into smaller and smaller profitable pieces — those forces fear one thing above all else: a people who remember how to talk to one another.

Wake up — not with fury alone. With a fierce, relentless, neighborly love.

Join The Wake
The Sound You've Been Looking For

Heavy Enough
to Feel It.
Honest Enough
to Mean It.

Four albums deep and still uncompromising. This project has always refused to make you choose between the pit and the message — music that hits as hard as it thinks, and thinks as hard as it hits.

Wake Up is the next chapter — and it might be the most urgent one yet.
